result:
Done. All three operations are implemented in `CsvRow.calr`, the project builds cleanly, and I verified the behavior end-to-end.

The source is written in Calor, a custom indentation-based DSL that compiles to C# via the project's SDK, so I first studied its syntax reference and compiler source to learn the constructs. Additions to `CsvRow.calr`, following the existing conventions (explicit IDs, compact signatures, effect declarations):

- **`EscapeField`** — wraps the field in quotes with internal quotes doubled (`replace field "\"" "\"\""`) when `NeedsQuoting` holds, returns it verbatim otherwise.
- **`JoinRow`** — folds over the list with `§EACH`, escaping each field and inserting commas between them; an empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** — a scanner with an outer field loop: quoted fields consume up to the un-doubled closing quote (doubled quotes contribute one literal quote; commas/CR/LF inside are content), unquoted fields run to the next comma or end of line, and a consumed comma at end of input produces a trailing empty field. The empty line parses to a single empty field.

Two Calor-specific details worth noting: an empty list can't be declared with a bare `§LIST` block (the parser needs elements or an explicit closer), so `SplitRow` binds one via `§NEW{List<str>}`; and `§PUSH` (i.e. `List.Add`) counts as a `mut` effect under the project's effects enforcement, so `SplitRow` declares `§E{mut}` — mutation is purely local, so the pure-computation constraint still holds. `NeedsQuoting` is untouched.

For verification I built a throwaway console project in `/tmp` (outside the source tree, deleted afterward) referencing the compiled `Src.dll`: 18 direct assertions covering the spec's edge cases (empty field/list, trailing comma, quoted commas/newlines/doubled quotes) plus 9 `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields))` round-trip cases with adversarial inputs — all passed. Total: 3 of the 10 build cycles used on the project itself.
